turns.content: I keep seeing these sanitizer flags in docs but I still don't get how to actually enable them properly. It seems like RUSTFLAGS is involved but the documentation isn't clear on how to combine AddressSanitizer, ThreadSanitizer, and MemorySanitizer into a single build configuration.

You're right that it gets confusing because you can't just pick one arbitrarily; they are distinct tools. AddressSanitizer catches memory safety issues like use-after-free and buffer overflows, while ThreadSanitizer detects data races between threads, and MemorySanitizer finds heap allocations that go out of scope or never freed. To enable multiple of them simultaneously in a single compilation you need to set RUSTFLAGS with the -Z sanitizer flags for each one you want.
So if I want both ASAN and TSAN enabled at once, do I just chain those flags together? Like RUSTFLAGS=-Z sanitizer=address,-Z sanitizer=thread?

That's actually the correct syntax for combining them. You would use RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=address -Z sanitizer=thread" in your build command or environment variable. However, there is a catch: not all sanitizers work well together because they both instrument memory operations which can interfere with each other.
Wait, so if I try to run both ASAN and TSAN in the same binary it might break things? Is there a way to make them coexist without breaking the program?

You are correct that running multiple sanitizers at once often causes false positives or crashes because they both track memory and thread state aggressively. The practical solution is to build separate binaries for each sanitizer test rather than trying to combine them in one executable. For example, compile with RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=address" for ASAN tests and another build with RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=thread" for TSAN tests.
But what about MemorySanitizer? Can I add that to one of these builds or do I need a third separate binary?

Yes, you can add MemorySanitizer to any single build by adding RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=memory" to the same command you use for AddressSanitizer or ThreadSanitizer. So a single build with RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=address -Z sanitizer=memory" will catch both memory leaks and use-after-free issues in one run.

Okay so my strategy is to create three separate cargo builds: ASAN, TSAN, and a combined ASAN+MemorySanitizer binary. That makes sense but I'm wondering if there's an easier way than maintaining multiple build configurations?

There isn't really a single flag that enables all three simultaneously because of the instrumentation conflicts, so maintaining separate builds is the recommended approach. However, you can automate this with a simple script or CI pipeline that runs cargo check with different RUSTFLAGS values for each test suite.

Ah got it! So I should just set up three separate cargo commands in my build script, one for ASAN, another for TSAN, and a third combining ASAN and MemorySanitizer. That's the only way to catch all these issues reliably.

Exactly! You will end up with three distinct binaries that each serve a specific purpose in your testing pipeline. This approach ensures you don't miss any type of memory safety issue while avoiding the conflicts that arise from running multiple sanitizers at once.

I just set up a new project and added the AddressSanitizer flags to my RUSTFLAGS environment variable, but I'm getting a compilation error saying I need to link against libstd. Can you help me fix this so I can run tests without errors?

The issue is that sanitizers require specific linking configurations that standard cargo doesn't always handle automatically. You should use the RUSTFLAGS environment variable with -Z sanitizer=address before running your command, or better yet, add a profile in your Cargo.toml file under [build] to set these flags globally for all builds. This ensures the compiler knows to include the necessary runtime components like libaddr and libunwind that handle the instrumentation.

Okay I added it to my build profile now it compiles but when I run the tests with cargo test -- --nocapture, I'm still getting a signal 11 error. It seems like the sanitizer isn't actually running or there's something wrong with how I configured the environment variables.

A signal 11 usually indicates an access violation which is exactly what AddressSanitizer catches when it finds a memory safety issue, but if you're seeing this consistently even with simple code, it might be that your build isn't actually compiling against the sanitizer. Ensure you are running cargo test -- -Z sanitizer=address explicitly or verify your RUSTFLAGS string includes -C link-arg=-l:addr and similar flags required by the compiler backend.

I checked my Cargo.toml and I have [build] set with RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=address" but it still fails. Could you explain why cargo isn't picking up these environment variables properly? Is there a better way to configure this for a development workflow?

Cargo does pick up environment variables, but sometimes the order of flags matters or specific build configurations override them. A more robust approach is to use the RUSTFLAGS variable in your shell before invoking cargo commands, or create a .cargo/config.toml file that defines custom profiles with these sanitizer flags pre-configured so you don't have to type them every time.

I tried creating a custom profile but it's still not working. I'm confused about whether RUSTFLAGS actually applies to the final binary or just intermediate steps, and if there are any specific compiler arguments needed besides what cargo passes automatically?

RUSTFLAGS does apply to the final compilation process when used correctly with environment variables, but you also need to ensure your project isn't using a profile that disables sanitizers. For AddressSanitizer specifically, you might want to check if your code contains any unsafe blocks or raw pointer operations that could be bypassing the instrumentation. It's like having a security guard at every door in a building; if someone finds a way through an unlocked window (unsafe code), they can slip past without being noticed.

That analogy makes sense, so I should avoid unsafe blocks? But what about ThreadSanitizer or MemorySanitizer? How do I enable those alongside AddressSanitizer and is there a single flag for all of them?

You can enable multiple sanitizers by combining flags like -Z sanitizer=address,thread,memory in your RUSTFLAGS string. However, be aware that running all three simultaneously might introduce overhead or conflicts depending on your target platform. Think of it like having three different types of sensors monitoring a building; using them together gives comprehensive coverage but requires careful calibration to avoid false positives from overlapping alerts.

I want to test my code thoroughly, so I'm thinking about running all sanitizers at once for maximum safety. But I'm worried about performance since we're in a startup environment and need fast iterations. Is there a way to selectively enable them based on the test type?

Yes, you can use conditional compilation macros or separate test suites with different configurations. For example, create a specific test binary that links with all sanitizers enabled while your main application uses only AddressSanitizer for runtime checks. This lets you run intensive tests in isolation without slowing down your daily development workflow.

That sounds like a good strategy but I'm still struggling to get the environment variables set correctly on my CI pipeline. Can you give me an example of how to configure the docker container or CI runner so it always has these sanitizer flags active by default?

In your CI configuration, export RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=address" before running cargo commands within the build step. For Docker containers specifically, add this line to your entrypoint script or as an environment variable in the dockerfile definition so every container instance inherits these flags automatically.
